sections.title: What are tenses?
sections.content:
In English, **tense** tells us **when** something happens: **three times × four aspects = 12** tense forms.
English doesn't change verb endings for each person (except 3rd person present: *he works*) — it builds the forms with auxiliary verbs.

diagrams.title: The auxiliary verbs that build them

sections.title: Simple aspect
sections.content: **Simple aspect** = fact, habit, or single completed event — no focus on duration or completion.

diagrams.title: Simple — structure & examples
rows: *I work every day. She likes coffee.*
*I will help you. She is going to study medicine.*

sections.title: Continuous aspect
sections.content:
**Continuous aspect** = action in progress at a given time.
Also used for temporary situations and background actions.

sections.title: Perfect aspect
sections.content: **Perfect aspect** = completed action with a result or relevance at a later time.

items.label: Structure
items.formula: have/has/had + past participle → I have finished

diagrams.title: Perfect — examples
rows: *I have finished. She has been to Japan.*

sections.title: Perfect continuous aspect
sections.content: **Perfect continuous aspect** = action lasting over a period, often with a visible result. Focus on **duration**.

items.label: Structure
items.formula: have/has/had been + -ing → I have been waiting

diagrams.title: Perfect continuous — examples
rows: *I have been waiting for an hour.*
*I had been waiting for an hour when she came.*
*By next month I will have been working here for ten years.*

sections.title: How to choose the right tense
sections.content:
Ask two questions:
- **When?** (past, present, future) → this gives you the **time**.
- **How do I view the action?** → this gives you the **aspect**.

steps.end: lasting over a period with a result → perfect continuous

sections.title: Typical errors
sections.content: The four classic mix-ups with the tense system.

items.good: **I work** every day. / **I am working** now.
items.why: simple for the habit, continuous for right now

items.good: **I have been waiting** for an hour.
items.bad: I am waiting for an hour.

sections.title: Reference
sections.content: For detailed rules on each tense, see the individual topics under Choose Grammar Topic (Past Simple, Present Perfect, Past Perfect, etc.).

questions.question: How do we get the 12 tense forms in English?
questions.explanation: We combine **3 times** (present, past, future) with **4 aspects** (simple, continuous, perfect, perfect continuous) to get 12 forms.

questions.question: In English, how do we usually form questions and negatives in most tenses?

options.a: By changing the verb ending for each person
options.b: By using auxiliary verbs (do, does, did, have, will, be, etc.)
options.c: By adding a suffix to the main verb
options.d: By changing the word order only

questions.explanation: English uses **auxiliary verbs** (do, does, did, have, has, had, will, am/is/are, was/were) to form questions, negatives, and many positive forms.

questions.question: What is **aspect** in English grammar?

options.a: The person (I, you, he/she) of the subject
options.b: The way we look at the action in time (e.g. in progress, completed)
options.c: The time of day when something happens
options.d: Whether the sentence is positive or negative

questions.explanation: **Aspect** is how we view the action in time: e.g. a single event, in progress, completed (with a result), or lasting over a period.

questions.question: Which aspect do we use when the action is **in progress** at a given time?
questions.explanation: **Continuous (progressive)** – the action is in progress. We use **be + -ing** (e.g. I am working, she was leaving).

questions.question: 'I have been waiting for an hour.' – Which tense form is this?
questions.explanation: **Present perfect continuous**: have/has been + -ing. We focus on how long something has been going on (duration).

questions.question: 'She had left before I arrived.' – Which tense form is this?
questions.explanation: **Past perfect simple**: had + past participle. Something completed **before** another time or action in the past.

questions.question: Which aspect focuses on **completion** and often on the **result** or relevance at a later time?
questions.explanation: **Perfect** – we focus on completion and result/relevance. We use have/has/had + past participle (e.g. I have finished, she had left).

questions.question: How does English typically express time and aspect?

options.a: English uses one word per tense.
options.b: English often uses auxiliaries + main verb (e.g. I have been waiting); time and aspect are shown by structure.

questions.explanation: **English** uses auxiliaries and often two or more words (e.g. I have been waiting). Time and aspect are shown by the verb structure, not by a single conjugated form.
